questions.question: Explain the concept of Remote Procedure Call and its primary advantages for distributed computing.
keyPoints: RPC allows execution of procedures on remote systems as if they were local
Abstracts network complexity from programmers
Enables writing distributed applications without socket programming
Provides location transparency

questions.question: Describe the role of Client Stub and Server Stub in RPC architecture.
keyPoints: Client Stub marshals parameters and sends request to server
Client Stub receives and unmarshals response
Server Stub receives request and unmarshals parameters
Server Stub calls actual procedure and marshals results back
Stubs hide network communication details from applications

questions.question: What is Data Marshalling and Unmarshalling in RPC? Why are they necessary?
keyPoints: Marshalling converts parameters to network-compatible format
Unmarshalling converts received data back to usable form
Necessary because machines have different data representations
Enables heterogeneous system communication
Also called serialization/deserialization

questions.question: Explain the Binding process in RPC and its importance.
keyPoints: Binding locates the server using directory service
Client obtains a handle to identify remote procedure
Enables dynamic server discovery
Supports load balancing and fault tolerance
Without binding, client cannot locate remote procedures

questions.question: Differentiate between Synchronous and Asynchronous RPC.
keyPoints: Synchronous RPC blocks client until response received
Asynchronous RPC allows client to continue without waiting
Asynchronous improves throughput and concurrency
Synchronous simpler to program, easier error handling
Choice depends on application requirements

questions.question: List and briefly explain three popular RPC implementations.
keyPoints: Sun RPC (ONC RPC): Uses XDR, widely used in Unix
DCE RPC: Provides security and authentication, cross-platform
gRPC: Modern, uses HTTP/2 and Protocol Buffers
XML-RPC: Uses XML for encoding, simple
JSON-RPC: Uses JSON, lightweight web-friendly

questions.question: What are the main challenges or disadvantages of using RPC in distributed systems?
keyPoints: Cannot easily handle network failures
Debugging distributed applications is difficult
Performance overhead due to marshalling/network latency
Partial failures hard to manage
Security concerns in untrusted networks
Lack of standardized semantics across implementations

questions.question: With a neat diagram, explain the five-layer architecture of RPC mechanism and the data flow from client application to server application.
keyPoints: Diagram showing: Application → Client Stub → RPC Runtime → Network → Server Stub → Server
Explain client stub marshals parameters and sends request
RPC Runtime handles message passing, authentication, retransmission
Server stub unmarshals parameters and calls procedure
Results travel back through same layers
Explain each layer's responsibility in detail
Discuss stub generation process using IDL

questions.question: Explain the various semantics (maybe-once, at-least-once, at-most-once) provided by RPC for handling failures. Under what circumstances does each semantic apply?
keyPoints: Maybe-once: Server executes zero or more times, used when idempotent operations
At-least-once: Server executes at least once, may cause duplicates, suitable for non-idempotent with compensating actions
At-most-once: Server executes exactly once or not at all, requires duplicate detection mechanisms
Explain client-side and server-side techniques for implementing each
Discuss trade-offs between simplicity and correctness
Give real-world examples of when each semantic is appropriate

questions.question: Compare and contrast Sun RPC, DCE RPC, and gRPC. Discuss their features, advantages, and typical use cases.
keyPoints: Sun RPC: Uses XDR, connectionless, simple, widely used in NFS
DCE RPC: Provides security, authentication, complex binding, used in enterprise environments
gRPC: Modern, HTTP/2 based, Protocol Buffers, supports streaming, microservices
Compare: Data format (XDR vs custom vs Protobuf)
Compare: Transport (UDP/TCP vs specific vs HTTP/2)
Compare: Language support and complexity
Use cases: Legacy systems (Sun/DCE), Modern cloud-native (gRPC)

questions.question: Explain the RPC mechanism in detail, including stub generation, binding, and how parameters are passed between client and server. Include a step-by-step explanation of a typical RPC call.
keyPoints: Step 1: Client calls local stub with parameters
Step 2: Client stub marshalls parameters using IDL-defined format
Step 3: Client stub sends message via RPC Runtime
Step 4: Network transports message to server
Step 5: Server stub receives and unmarshalls parameters
Step 6: Server stub calls actual procedure
Step 7: Procedure executes and returns result
Step 8: Server stub marshalls result and sends back
Step 9: Client stub receives and unmarshalls result
Step 10: Client application receives result
Explain stub generation from IDL
Explain binding process for server discovery
